narrativeSummary: Version 3.5 overhauls the project detail page with a structured two-column card layout, adds a full-featured sortable and paginated dashboard table, introduces read-only intake viewing from the FS-1 phase, and fixes the FS-1 review board to use the unified project_stage field. The project detail overview is now a clean two-column card with left column showing project metadata (origin, type, dates, contact officer) and right column showing financial details (cost breakdown, funding progress bar, pathway). The dashboard “Recent Submissions” table now shows all projects with eight sortable columns, full pagination, and middle-click-to-open-in-new-tab. The appraisal wizard lets users view their intake data read-only from the FS-1 phase via the progress rail, with a yellow banner and “Back to Feasibility Study” button. The PFS form now shows a validation error summary that maps errors to their tabs with clickable links. Contact officer fields have moved inline under the Project Origin section. The FS-1 review board and review API now use project_stage instead of feasibility_stage, fixing stage-mismatch bugs. New Project Bank badge variants have been added to the design system, and all metadata cards across review tabs use a stacked layout for implementing agency and sub-sector instead of inline dots.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release focuses on making project data more accessible, improving the appraisal workflow, and fixing review board stage handling. The project detail page has been completely restructured: the overview tab now uses a two-column card layout with project metadata on the left (origin, type, PPP contract type, estimated start date, duration, description, objectives, beneficiaries, contact officer, SDGs, MSDP alignment) and financial details on the right (cost breakdown chart, funding progress bar, pathway, categorisation). The collapsible PFS sections have been removed in favour of this cleaner layout. The dashboard “Recent Submissions” table has been expanded from a simple five-row preview to a full data table fetching all projects with eight sortable columns (Project Title, Sector, Ministry/Agency, Project Type, State/Region, Estimated Start Date, Estimated Cost, Status), client-side pagination with configurable page sizes, and sortable column headers with ascending/descending indicators. Rows support middle-click and Ctrl/Cmd-click to open in new tabs. The appraisal wizard now supports read-only intake viewing when working in the FS-1 phase: clicking the “Intake” step in the progress rail shows the intake form greyed out with a yellow “Viewing intake data (read-only)” banner and a “Back to Feasibility Study” button. The viability sidebar and action bar are hidden during intake viewing. The progress rail shows the project name at the top and the FS-1 phase heading is clickable to return to the current phase. The PFS form now displays a validation error summary below the cost table, mapping each error to its tab with clickable links that switch to the relevant tab. The contact officer section has been moved from a standalone section at the bottom of the intake form to an inline card directly under the Project Origin cards, with the proponent section shown for unsolicited proposals. The FS-1 review board API has been fixed to query project_stage instead of feasibility_stage, and the FS-1 review endpoint now reads project_stage for tier validation and maps the “screened” decision to fs1_desk_screened correctly. The submit endpoint now syncs feasibility_stage when submitting from FS-1. The wizard auto-transitions project_stage from intake_approved to fs1_draft on first FS-1 save. Seven new Project Bank badge variants have been added to the design system (pb-entry, pb-progress, pb-review, pb-approved, pb-active, pb-done, pb-rejected) and status badges on the dashboard and project list use these variants. The funding gap hero card now shows the number of projects with gaps. Metadata in review board Kanban cards uses a stacked layout (implementing agency and sub-sector on separate lines) instead of inline dot separators. The funding gaps page now has sortable column headers across all columns. The date picker uses the full date format (e.g. “4 March 2026”). Currency formatting always shows the code prefix (e.g. “USD”) instead of “$”. A seed script provides sample Project Bank data for development environments.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release extends the two-tier review model to all review board phases and adds richer metadata throughout the project pipeline. The Intake Review Board now supports a full desk/senior workflow: desk screeners can pass, return, or reject submissions, and projects that pass desk screening advance to a senior review stage before final approval. A new intake_desk_screened project stage tracks this intermediate state. The FS-2 Review Board has been expanded with three new stages — fs2_desk_reviewed, fs2_senior_reviewed, and fs2_returned — enabling the same desk-then-senior pattern for detailed feasibility studies. The categorisation endpoint now accepts projects from fs2_senior_reviewed in addition to fs2_completed. The Intake Review Board gains a Kanban view with three columns (Desk Review, Senior Review, Returned), complementing the existing card, list, and table views. All review board tabs are now labelled with phase numbers (Phase 1, Phase 2, Phase 3). The status timeline stepper labels each phase with its number and, for Phase 4, shows the appropriate pathway label based on the category decision (Private Sector, Government Budget, or PPP Transaction Structuring). Every project card across all four review tabs (Intake, FS-1, FS-2, Rejected) now displays the implementing agency below the ministry and the sub-sector below the sector. The same secondary metadata has been added to the project list table and the funding gaps table. Currency values in table columns now use a split prefix/amount format via a new formatCurrencyParts() utility, showing the currency code in muted text and the amount separately for consistent visual alignment. Filter dropdowns on the project list now display short codes next to labels (status codes 1–5, sector codes, origin codes GOV/UNS, pathway codes ODA/PPP/PVS/PVT/DOM). Column headers across the project list use full labels (“Project Title”, “Nominating Ministry”, “Estimated Cost”). On the project detail page, the interactive FS-1 narrative form has been replaced with a read-only FS1NarrativeDisplay component that fetches and renders all five narrative sections. A read-only cash flow projection table is now shown on the feasibility tab when cost data exists. The legacy “Advance to” status button has been removed — all stage transitions now go through the review board. The appraisal wizard action button uses a prominent dark (black) style. The overview section uses simple spacing instead of divider lines. All badge styles for both the FEASIBILITY_STAGE_BADGE_STYLES and PROJECT_STAGE_BADGE_STYLES maps have been normalised to the five-colour system palette (Scarlet, Pale Slate, Blue Slate, Cool Steel, Platinum), eliminating the previous mix of Tailwind greens, purples, indigos, and yellows. The rejected projects API and review board API queries now include implementing_agency and sub_sector fields. A new /api/project-bank/review-board/intake endpoint serves the Intake Kanban columns. A database migration adds the intake_reviews table and new review board stages.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release focuses on two themes: strengthening the Preliminary Feasibility Study workflow and making project data more visible at a glance. The Review Board page has been completely refactored — the monolithic 1,100-line file has been split into four focused tabs: Intake Reviews, FS-1 Reviews, FS-2 Reviews, and a new Rejected Projects tab that shows rejected projects with their rejection reasons and cool-down timers. Each tab is now its own component for maintainability. The project detail page has been substantially expanded: when a project has Preliminary Feasibility Study data, it now displays that data in collapsible sections covering Technical (approach, methodology, risks, design maturity), Revenue (sources, projections, market assessment), Environmental & Social (impact levels with descriptions, land acquisition and resettlement requirements), and Financial Analysis (FIRR, NPV, payback period with inline metric cards). The status timeline has been redesigned from a seven-step model (nominated, screening, appraisal, approved, implementation, completed, rejected) to a clean four-phase stepper (Intake, FS-1, FS-2, FS-3) with completed checkmarks. During FS-1, a sticky Viability Decision sidebar now floats on the right side of the appraisal wizard, showing the routing recommendation (Private, PPP, Government Budget) in real time as the user enters FIRR data. The PFS form now validates that the feasibility study conductor is specified, at least one cost row exists, and environmental and social impact levels are selected before submission. Revenue sources use a multi-select popover with predefined options (toll fees, user charges, lease income, etc.) instead of free-form text, and an “Other” option reveals a description field. Environmental and social impact fields now include description text areas that appear when an impact level is selected. Six new document types have been added — Environmental Impact Assessment, Social Impact Assessment, Land Acquisition Plan, Resettlement Plan, Technical Design, and Market Assessment — and each PFS tab now has its own document upload zone scoped to relevant types. Documents can now be renamed and reclassified inline via a new PATCH endpoint and edit popover. The dashboard has gained count/value toggle buttons on all three charts (pipeline status, pathway, sector), allowing users to switch between viewing project counts and pipeline values. Sector charts now group entries beyond the top five into an “Other” bucket, and tooltips on all charts show both count and value simultaneously. The FS-1 Kanban board columns have been simplified to Submitted, Desk Screened, and Returned (removing the combined Decided column). Sensitivity analysis scenarios now include explanatory tooltips, and revenue projection fields have been moved from the Revenue tab to the Financial Analysis page. The origin cards on the intake form now use background images, and the FundingGapBar uses proportional-width numerals for cleaner alignment.

releases.narrativeSummary: This is a governance-focused release that transforms how projects move through the pipeline. Instead of a flat list of appraisal stages, every project now follows a formal phase-gate model: Intake → FS-1 → FS-2 → FS-3, with the review board acting as a gatekeeper between each phase. When a line ministry finishes filling out an intake form, they click “Submit for Review”, which locks the form and sends it to the review board. Reviewers can approve (unlocking the next phase), return for revision (with comments the submitter can see), or reject. The FS-1 Review Board now supports drag-and-drop — reviewers can drag project cards between Kanban columns — and a gate checklist requires reviewers to verify the narrative, NDP alignment, and cost estimate before passing a project to FS-2. The appraisal wizard has been restructured to match: a redesigned progress rail shows exactly which phase and gate you are at, MSDP screening is now part of the intake form rather than a separate step, and financial analysis (FIRR) is embedded within FS-1. The FIRR page now shows a side-by-side comparison of preliminary cost estimates versus refined figures, with percentage-change indicators and a grouped bar chart. Cash flow entry has been improved with formatted number inputs (commas while typing), quick-entry distribution periods (construction vs operational years), and an integrated chart with annual and cumulative views. The intake form itself has been significantly enhanced: projects can have banner images, origin selection uses illustrated cards instead of a dropdown, ministry and department fields cascade from the chart of accounts, project types and sectors are now managed by administrators through a new admin tab rather than being hardcoded, and regions are grouped by States, Regions, and Union Territories. On the Land Bank side, parcels can now have banner images (starred from the document list), the map supports polyline drawing alongside polygons, location fields use cascading State/Township selects, and NDP goals are selected via clickable cards instead of a dropdown. All sixteen analytics endpoints now exclude internal transfers (pooled fund onward flows) to prevent double-counting, backed by a database-level view update. Data tables across all four modules (Activities, Transactions, Budgets, Planned Disbursements) now use full-width auto-sizing instead of fixed column widths. The entire application has been normalised to a consistent neutral colour palette, replacing the previous mix of blue, purple, and green accents with a cohesive earth-toned scheme.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release adds a complete feasibility study lifecycle to the Project Bank. Every new project now progresses through three stages: FS-1 (Preliminary Screening), where line ministries write a structured narrative covering the problem statement, beneficiaries, NDP alignment, expected outcomes, and cost justification — each section requiring at least 200 words to ensure rigour. Submissions are reviewed on a new Kanban-style Review Board where screening officers process projects through desk review and senior review tiers, with mandatory comments for any rejection or return. A six-month cool-down prevents resubmission of rejected projects from the same ministry. Projects that pass FS-1 move to FS-2 (Detailed Feasibility Study), where a consultant is assigned with a deadline and progress tracking. When the study completes, FIRR and EIRR results are recorded and fed into an algorithmic categorisation engine that recommends Category A (fully private, FIRR ≥ 10%), Category B (government budget), or Category C (PPP, for NDP-aligned projects where FIRR is low but EIRR is strong). Decision-makers can override the recommendation with a mandatory written rationale. PPP structuring has been expanded beyond VGF to include Minimum Revenue Guarantees (with liability caps and trigger conditions) and Availability Payments (with annual amounts and durations). The standalone PPP Pipeline page has been retired — PPP projects are now identified through the categorisation workflow and visible as a column in the main Project List. Across all Project Bank tables, status badges now use a unified monochrome palette, project codes appear as copyable chips, rows feature kebab action menus on hover, and sort columns use arrow icons instead of text characters. The sidebar has been reorganised: “Project Bank” is now “Projects”, the Review Board has its own link, Monitoring is elevated to its own navigation group, and the PPP Pipeline link has been removed.

releases.narrativeSummary: This patch improves the reliability of the Land Bank module. When the system was first deployed, certain reference tables — such as the line ministries lookup and the parcel documents store — might not yet exist in every environment. Previously this could cause the parcel list, parcel detail, and statistics pages to return errors. Now all three Land Bank API endpoints detect when a join fails and automatically retry with a simpler query, so you always see your data even if optional tables haven’t been created yet. The parcel list endpoint also received a refactored filter pipeline for cleaner, more maintainable code.

Parcel detail endpoint: primary query now attempts joins to line_ministries and national_development_goals with automatic fallback; documents query wrapped in try/catch for environments where land_parcel_documents doesn’t exist yet
Statistics endpoint: aggregation query retries without the line_ministries join if it fails, ensuring the dashboard always loads

releases.narrativeSummary: This is a major milestone. The Project Bank is no longer just a submission-and-appraisal pipeline — it now covers the full public-investment lifecycle from nomination through to post-approval monitoring. Projects can specify one of seven PPP contract types aligned with Notification 2/2018 (Availability Payment, BOO, BOT, BTL, BTO, O&M, Other), each with its own detail form covering concession periods, equity ratios, and KPI frameworks. Equity ratio compliance is validated automatically against configurable thresholds (30% for projects under $50M, 20% above). Unsolicited proposals enter through a dedicated origin pathway with proponent details and a Swiss Challenge tab for managing the competitive bidding process. Projects above a configurable dollar threshold cannot reach “approved” status without a cabinet-approval document on file. Once approved, a Monitoring tab appears with scheduled review cadences and report submissions. A new SEE Transfers section tracks State Economic Enterprise equitisations with yearly financials and document uploads. Projects and land parcels are now cross-linked — the project detail sidebar suggests matching parcels, and the parcel detail sidebar suggests matching projects. On the Land Bank side, every parcel now carries a title status (Clear, Pending Verification, Under Review, Disputed, Unregistered), an asset type, a controlling ministry, and NDP goal alignment. Parcel documents can be uploaded and managed from a new Documents tab. The analytics dashboard gains three new chart dimensions: by asset type, by title status, and by ministry. The parcels list page has filters for all new dimensions. Working Groups gain document editing, meeting deletion, and member-count enrichment. The home page now renders inside the standard sidebar layout, and cross-platform routes like FAQ, Build History, and Admin inherit the sidebar context of the module you were last in.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release brings the Land Bank module online and significantly polishes the Project Bank. The Land Bank gets a full analytics dashboard with stat cards, status breakdown, classification pie chart, parcels-by-region bar chart, and a recently-added-parcels table, plus its own sidebar navigation group with items for Dashboard, All Parcels, Register New, Import Parcels, and Analytics. In the Project Bank, the dashboard’s hand-drawn CSS bar charts have been replaced with proper Recharts horizontal bar charts, the project list gains a funding gap progress bar column and a unified filter bar, and the appraisal wizard has been overhauled — every form label now carries a contextual help tooltip, the progress rail shows sub-stage navigation with scroll-to anchors, and the results panels for FIRR and EIRR feature sidebar layouts with hero metric cards, stacked cost/revenue bar charts, and tornado sensitivity-analysis charts. The home page now renders as a standalone full-screen portal (no sidebar wrapper), the module card renamed from “AIMS” to “DFMIS”, and a module switcher has been added to the top-nav user dropdown so you can jump between modules from anywhere. Permissions have been expanded with canCreateParcels, canManageParcels, and canRequestAllocation, with government and development partner roles receiving different land-related capabilities. The land parcels database schema has been overhauled with renamed fields and four new supporting tables.

releases.narrativeSummary: This is the foundation for turning the system from a single AIMS application into a multi-module platform. The sidebar now detects which module you are in based on the URL — /project-bank/* shows the Project Bank navigation, everything else shows the familiar AIMS navigation — and a “Back to Home” link at the top lets you jump between modules. The Project Bank sidebar includes Dashboard, All Projects, Submit New, Funding Gaps, and PPP Pipeline items, with its own “Submit Project” CTA button gated by a new canCreateProjects permission. On the AIMS side, the activity list gains an Origin column that shows whether each activity was donor-reported or came from the Project Bank, displayed as colour-coded badges. Behind the scenes, full TypeScript types have been added for the new database tables: project_bank_projects, project_bank_donors, project_appraisals, project_documents, appraisal_shadow_prices, land_parcels, and national_development_goals. The admin panel gains an NDP Goals tab for managing national development plan goals. The home route now always points to /home (the entry portal) rather than conditionally routing based on organisation assignment, and getCurrentModule() is the new utility that powers the module-aware navigation.

releases.narrativeSummary: The Aid Effectiveness admin panel has been expanded so each government planning document can carry an acronym and a start/end date with configurable precision — year only, month and year, or full date. Those details now appear in the option list and in the Aid Effectiveness survey dropdowns, making it easy to distinguish between overlapping national plans. Pooled fund cards on the Funds page now display the activity’s banner image across the top of the card if one has been set, and the date range at the bottom has been upgraded from a plain text line to a two-column layout with calendar icons. The card is no longer a giant click target — only the title link navigates, which removes all the stopPropagation hacks that were scattered through the card’s event handlers. Three card-view action menus (Activity Card, Organisation Card, and Task Card) that still relied on the third-party bloom-menu library have been replaced with the standard DropdownMenu component used everywhere else in the system, giving them consistent styling and keyboard navigation. The dashboard hero chart-view toggle (bar/line/table) has been updated to use the same subtle slate highlight as the pagination buttons rather than a filled primary button. Smaller fixes include removing font-mono from sector percentages, upgrading the sector aggregation preview to use a code badge for sector codes, a bar-radius fix on the Funding by Modality chart, and a minor identifier styling tweak in the activity editor.

releases.narrativeSummary: When you opened an activity, the sidebar tabs would all appear empty until you actually clicked into each section and its data finished loading. Now the basic API response includes lightweight record counts for every tab, so completion ticks show up the moment the page loads — no more guessing which sections already have data. The SDG Alignment section, which used to be a sprawling two-column grid of large cards, has been replaced with a compact summary table; clicking any row or an SDG tile opens a dialog where you configure alignment strength, targets, and notes, then close it when you’re done. Contact search now pulls from both the contacts table and the users table so you can find anyone in the system, and it shows results on focus before you start typing. Pagination buttons everywhere — Activities, Transactions, Budgets, Library, FAQ, Build History, and more — now use a subtle slate highlight instead of a filled primary button for the active page. The Focal Points tab has been rebuilt to use the same PersonCard component as the Rolodex, the Humanitarian tab moves the “Add Scope” button into the header area for quicker access, and several analytics charts have had their tooltip and axis formatting tidied up. The fund summary calculation has been corrected so contributions show the most advanced pipeline stage rather than double-counting received plus committed plus pledged amounts. Finally, the dashboard API now excludes internal transfer transactions from totals to prevent inflated figures.

releases.narrativeSummary: This release is about visual consistency and code hygiene. Every dialog and modal in the system — from adding transactions and contacts to editing organisations, tasks, documents, and meetings — now opens with the same muted-background header bar and bottom border, so they all feel like they belong to the same application. Filter labels across analytics pages, the library, admin panels, and user management have been simplified from bold slate headings to smaller, quieter muted text so your eye goes to the data, not the chrome. Behind the scenes, the activity-status display logic that was duplicated in five different files has been pulled into a single shared utility, which means status badges now look identical everywhere — activity cards, the activity editor, and the pooled funds page — and any future status-related change only needs to happen in one place. We also removed the redundant “Story So Far” summary card from the Build History page; the per-release narrative card at the top already tells you everything you need to know.

releases.narrativeSummary: Tables across the system were starting to feel uneven — some columns too wide, others too narrow, and the title column would sometimes get squeezed when lots of columns were visible. We switched every data table (Activities, Transactions, Budgets, and Planned Disbursements) to a fixed-layout approach where each column type gets an appropriate width and the title column stretches to fill whatever space is left, so the layout stays balanced at any screen size. A thin filler column at the right edge ensures that row hover highlights extend all the way to the container boundary. On the Pooled Funds side, the Sankey chart now only shows a third tier of child activities when there are actual disbursement values to display, and hovering over any flow link pops up a tooltip showing the donor or child name and dollar amount. We also moved the fund status badge underneath the IATI identifier for a cleaner card layout, made fund titles clickable links to the fund overview, and swapped the green colour scheme to match the system’s red primary palette. In the sidebar, items previously marked “New” have been moved to the standard under-development indicator for consistency, with only Pooled Funds retaining a “New” badge.

releases.narrativeSummary: We tidied up the tables across æther so they feel more polished and consistent. Previously, the action menu on the activity list used a custom animated component that caused the kebab button to sit out of alignment with the rest of the row — when you hovered over a row the highlight would stop short, leaving an awkward white gap on the right. We replaced that menu with the same straightforward dropdown used elsewhere in the system, removed the now-unnecessary Actions column header from both the Activities and Transactions tables, and made sure that hovering over any row in any table — Activities, Transactions, Budgets, or Planned Disbursements — highlights the entire row edge to edge. Small details, but they add up to a cleaner, more trustworthy interface.

releases.narrativeSummary: This update prepares the system for pooled and trust funds: activities can now be marked as pooled funds and linked to child activities, with seed data to demonstrate the flow of contributions and disbursements. On the Aid Effectiveness side, each dropdown option can be linked to responsible ministries, making it easier to see which government bodies own which choices. Seed data for all four Aid Effectiveness dropdown categories has been added so countries can go live with realistic options for national plans, results frameworks, accountability, and capacity development.
